explicit_text: My name is Jerry Bitz, born in Portland, Oregon on June 26, 1941. I started working on the waterfront as a casual in 1959, became a Class B in 1964, and a registered A longshoreman in 1968. Over my 42 years on the waterfront, I worked for ILWU Locals 8 and 40, held leadership roles including president of both locals, and was deeply involved in union politics and labor relations. My experience ranged from handling hides and lumber to driving cranes and training others, and I always felt blessed to be part of the ILWU family.
implicit_text: My name is Jerry, born in Portland, Oregon on June 26, 1941. I started working on the waterfront as a casual in 1959, became a Class B in 1964, and a registered A longshoreman in 1968. Over my 42 years on the waterfront, I worked for two local unions, held leadership roles including president of both locals, and was deeply involved in union politics and labor relations. My experience ranged from handling hides and lumber to driving cranes and training others, and I always felt blessed to be part of the waterfront family.

explicit_text: The International Longshore and Warehouse Union, or ILWU, has been central to my life and career on the waterfront. I joined the ILWU as a casual worker in 1959 and later became a registered longshoreman. The union fostered a strong sense of camaraderie and family among its members, and I was proud to serve in leadership roles within ILWU Locals 8 and 40. The ILWU also played a key role during significant strikes and labor negotiations, shaping the waterfront's working conditions over the decades.
implicit_text: The union representing longshore and warehouse workers has been central to my life and career on the waterfront. I joined as a casual worker in 1959 and later became a registered longshoreman. The organization fostered a strong sense of camaraderie and family among its members, and I was proud to serve in leadership roles within two local branches. The union also played a key role during significant strikes and labor negotiations, shaping the waterfront's working conditions over the decades.

explicit_text: I was born in Portland, Oregon, in 1941, though I actually grew up in Shorewood, a suburb south of Portland. Portland was the hub of my waterfront career, where I worked as a longshoreman and was active in the ILWU. The city’s port was where I experienced many aspects of the job, from handling lumber to driving cranes, and where I participated in union activities and conventions. Portland’s waterfront shaped much of my working life and union involvement.
implicit_text: I was born in Portland, Oregon, in 1941, though I actually grew up in a suburb south of the city. Portland was the hub of my waterfront career, where I worked as a longshoreman and was active in the union. The city’s port was where I experienced many aspects of the job, from handling lumber to driving cranes, and where I participated in union activities and conventions. Portland’s waterfront shaped much of my working life and union involvement.

explicit_text: Though I was born in Portland, I actually grew up in Shorewood, Oregon, about 18 miles south of the city. We commuted daily to Portland for work, especially when I started as a casual on the waterfront. Growing up in Shorewood meant a suburban life but with strong ties to the waterfront community in Portland. My family, including my father who was also a longshoreman, lived there during the 1948 strike and beyond.
implicit_text: Though I was born in Portland, I actually grew up in a small community about 18 miles south of the city. We commuted daily to Portland for work, especially when I started as a casual on the waterfront. Growing up there meant a suburban life but with strong ties to the waterfront community in Portland. My family, including my father who was also a longshoreman, lived there during the 1948 strike and beyond.

explicit_text: The 1948 West Coast waterfront strike was a defining event for my family and me. My father was a longshoreman working nights during that time, and I was about 11 or 12 years old. I remember the struggle as benefits were scarce and we had to rely on community support, like food baskets from our church. Though I hid in my room when the charity came, I now feel proud of how the strike and the waterfront shaped our family’s resilience and future.

explicit_text: I attended and graduated from Tigard High School in 1960. It was the local high school near where I lived in Shorewood, Oregon. After graduating, I chose to work on the waterfront rather than serve in the military, which was an important decision in my life. Tigard High was part of my early years before I fully immersed myself in the ILWU and waterfront work.

explicit_text: During my waterfront career, I drove cranes at Schnitzer Steel Industries, among other places. While I never took a formal crane operator plug, I often stepped in to drive cranes on scrap at Schnitzer when needed. This experience was part of the variety of jobs I did, from handling lumber to operating heavy equipment, which made my work on the waterfront diverse and rewarding.
implicit_text: During my waterfront career, I drove cranes at a major steel recycling company, among other places. While I never took a formal crane operator plug, I often stepped in to drive cranes on scrap when needed. This experience was part of the variety of jobs I did, from handling lumber to operating heavy equipment, which made my work on the waterfront diverse and rewarding.

explicit_text: In 1978, I attended a union caucus delegate convention in San Diego, which was quite an adventure for me. I was impressed by how the Hawaiian locals hosted their members with a 24/7 hospitality room, showing great care for their own. This experience deepened my interest in union politics and inspired me to become more active in ILWU leadership over the years.
implicit_text: In 1978, I attended a union caucus delegate convention in a southern California city, which was quite an adventure for me. I was impressed by how the locals from the islands hosted their members with a 24/7 hospitality room, showing great care for their own. This experience deepened my interest in union politics and inspired me to become more active in leadership over the years.

explicit_text: Though I did not mention Harry Bridges directly in detail, as a longshoreman and ILWU member during the decades following his leadership, his legacy was certainly felt. Harry Bridges was a foundational figure in the ILWU, and his influence shaped the union culture and labor struggles that I experienced on the waterfront. His role in the union’s history provided the backdrop for much of the labor activism I participated in.

explicit_text: While I did not provide detailed personal stories about the 1971 West Coast longshore strike, it was one of the major labor events that shaped the waterfront during my career. As an active ILWU member and later a union leader, I witnessed the impact of such strikes on workers’ rights and benefits. These strikes were part of the ongoing struggle to improve conditions on the docks where I worked for decades.

explicit_text: The Taft–Hartley Act was part of the broader legal and political context affecting union activities during my time on the waterfront. Although I did not discuss it in detail, this act influenced how unions like the ILWU operated and negotiated. Understanding its impact was important as we navigated labor relations and strikes throughout my career.
implicit_text: The federal labor law passed in 1947 was part of the broader legal and political context affecting union activities during my time on the waterfront. Although I did not discuss it in detail, this act influenced how unions like mine operated and negotiated. Understanding its impact was important as we navigated labor relations and strikes throughout my career.

explicit_text: The Pacific Maritime Association was the employer organization we negotiated with during my years on the waterfront. They represented the shipping companies and terminal operators, and much of my union work involved dealing with them in arbitrations and contract talks. Our interactions with the PMA were central to securing better wages and working conditions for ILWU members.
implicit_text: The employer association representing shipping companies and terminal operators was the organization we negotiated with during my years on the waterfront. Much of my union work involved dealing with them in arbitrations and contract talks. Our interactions with this group were central to securing better wages and working conditions for union members.

explicit_text: I worked in Vancouver handling lumber, which was one of the more physically demanding but rewarding jobs on the waterfront. Early on, we hand-stowed lumber piece by piece before mechanization improved the process. Working in Vancouver gave me a chance to experience different types of cargo and contributed to the variety that made longshore work so unique.

explicit_text: The Port of Portland was the center of my longshore career and union activities. It was where I worked for many years as a longshoreman and later as a union leader in ILWU Local 8. The port saw many changes over the decades, including mechanization and labor struggles, and it was the community where I built lifelong relationships and contributed to the waterfront’s legacy.
